next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

By the time Marisol got off the B41, the cuffs of her jeans were wet and black from slush, and the paper folder under her coat had started to curl at the corners from her body heat. She had printed the release packet at the copy shop in three sets, ten-point font, legal margins, every clause she had fought over with a borrowed template and two nights of insomnia. The top page still smelled faintly of toner and the hand lotion she kept behind the register to stop her fingers from cracking. On the back of the folder she had written, in thick blue marker, the only instruction that mattered: GET THE SIGNATURE BEFORE MORPHINE.

She stood in front of Methodist's palliative entrance and looked at her reflection in the black glass. She saw a runner-thin face, hair braided badly because she had done it in the dark at six, and a burn scar near her left wrist half-hidden by the cuff of her sweater. She took out her phone. A text from her landlord sat on top of everything else, timestamped 7:12 a.m.: `Need at least $600 today or I post notice.` Under that, a group thread from Carina with Leo's latest pigeon drawing and no direct message to her. Under that, nothing from Ada.

Inside, the air changed from wet December metal to over-clean citrus and old fear. At the desk, a volunteer handed her a flimsy sticker with VISITOR in red block letters and told her to keep it visible. The volunteer had gray braids wrapped in a Yankees scarf and a voice like she sang in church, and she called everyone baby in exactly the same tone, even the man cursing at the vending machine down the...
open_hooks_to_carry: Quiet domestic scenes include homework, bus delays, and cheap takeout without strategic agenda.
Leo asks direct child-logic questions about saints and liars.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...sh, only terms. Marisol appreciated the clarity so much it made her throat sting.

She set three alarms and kept writing until after midnight, shaping transitions, trimming verdict words, checking every assertion against paper evidence. At 12:47 a.m. she printed the full draft, fourteen pages now, and spread them across the floor. She crawled from page to page with a pencil, circling any sentence that told the reader what to feel instead of showing the cost. She crossed out nine circles.

At 1:30 she made a final pass for verbs. She replaced was betrayed with learned. She replaced was destroyed with paid and hid and drank and posted. She replaced villain with witness twice removed. She left one sentence ugly on purpose because the polished version lied: "I wanted him to suffer before he died."

That sentence terrified her. It also made the rest of the chapter honest enough to stand.

At 1:58 she saved the document as "catalogue_essay_v8_SUBMIT_after_interview.docx" and exported a PDF copy to guard against panic edits in the morning. She slid the printed stack into a manila envelope with the death certificate copy, the shift log excerpts, and the blue day-one chip she carried like a small hard planet in her pocket.

She did not pray. She sat on the edge of the bed with her coat still on and listened to the radiator knock the same uneven rhythm as before, like someone asking to be let in and then changing their mind.

Tomorrow she would sit across from a dying man whose lucid windows were closing like elevator doors. Tonight she had a draft that no longer pretended innocence.
state_deltas_required: Re-enter ordinary family life long enough to test whether reconciliation is possible.
Marisol learns Gloria pawned her wedding ring to fund Marisol's first rehab stay, complicating her moral framing of Gloria.
